questions.question: What is the left ventricle?
options: Heart chamber that pumps blood to the body, known as the workhorse of the heart
Chamber of the heart that pumps blood to the lungs
Chamber of the heart that receives blood from the body
Heart chamber that receives blood from the lungs
questions.explanation: The left ventricle has the thickest muscular wall of all four chambers because it has to generate enough force to push blood through the entire systemic circulation — hence 'the workhorse of the heart.'

questions.question: What is the tricuspid valve?
options: Type of valve located in the aorta and pulmonary artery
Atrioventricular valve between the left atrium and left ventricle
Valve located between the right atrium and right ventricle
Artery that transports blood to the entire body
questions.explanation: The tricuspid valve sits on the right side of the heart, between the right atrium and right ventricle. Its counterpart on the left side is the mitral (bicuspid) valve.

questions.question: What is the left atrium?
options: Heart chamber that receives blood from the lungs
Chamber of the heart that receives blood from the body
Heart chamber that pumps blood to the body
Chamber of the heart that pumps blood to the lungs
questions.explanation: The left atrium is the receiving chamber for freshly oxygenated blood coming from the lungs via the pulmonary veins, before passing it to the left ventricle.

questions.question: What is the aorta?
options: Artery that transports blood to the entire body
Blood vessel that provides a pathway for deoxygenated blood to return to the lungs
Blood vessel that transports blood from the lungs to the left atrium
Type of valve located in the aorta and pulmonary artery
questions.explanation: The aorta is the body's largest artery — it carries freshly oxygenated blood from the left ventricle out to systemic circulation, reaching every part of the body.

questions.question: What is the pulmonary trunk?
options: Artery that transports blood to the entire body
Blood vessel that provides a pathway for deoxygenated blood to return to the lungs
Blood vessel that transports blood from the lungs to the left atrium
Chamber of the heart that pumps blood to the lungs
questions.explanation: The pulmonary trunk carries deoxygenated blood away from the right ventricle toward the lungs, where it will pick up oxygen — the opposite direction and oxygen content of the aorta.

questions.question: What is the right atrium?
options: Chamber of the heart that pumps blood to the lungs
Heart chamber that receives blood from the lungs
Chamber of the heart that receives blood from the body
Heart chamber that pumps blood to the body
questions.explanation: The right atrium is the entry point for deoxygenated blood returning from the body via the superior and inferior vena cava, before it moves on to the right ventricle.

questions.question: What is the right ventricle?
options: Heart chamber that pumps blood to the body
Chamber of the heart that pumps blood to the lungs
Chamber of the heart that receives blood from the body
Heart chamber that receives blood from the lungs
questions.explanation: The right ventricle pumps deoxygenated blood into the pulmonary trunk toward the lungs — a much shorter, lower-pressure circuit than the left ventricle's job of pumping to the entire body.

questions.question: What is a semilunar valve?
options: Atrioventricular valve between the left atrium and left ventricle
Valve located between the right atrium and right ventricle
Type of valve located in the aorta and the pulmonary artery
Artery that transports blood to the entire body
questions.explanation: Semilunar valves sit at the exits of the ventricles — the aortic valve and pulmonic valve — preventing backflow into the ventricles after each contraction.

questions.question: What is the pulmonary vein?
options: Blood vessel that provides a pathway for deoxygenated blood to return to the lungs
Blood vessel that transports blood from the lungs to the left atrium
Artery that transports blood to the entire body
Heart chamber that pumps blood to the lungs
questions.explanation: The pulmonary vein carries freshly oxygenated blood from the lungs back to the left atrium — notably, it's a vein carrying oxygenated blood, which is the reverse of how most veins work.

questions.question: What is the mitral (bicuspid) valve?
options: Valve located between the right atrium and right ventricle
Type of valve located in the aorta and pulmonary artery
Blood vessel that transports blood from the lungs to the left atrium
Atrioventricular valve between the left atrium and left ventricle
questions.explanation: The mitral valve is the left-side counterpart to the tricuspid valve — it sits between the left atrium and left ventricle, controlling blood flow into the heart's most powerful chamber.

questions.question: What is excitability, as a property of heart cells?
options: Ability of the heart muscle cells to respond to an impulse or stimulus
Ability of the heart to initiate an electrical impulse
Ability of the heart muscle cells to shorten in response to an electrical stimulus
Ability of the heart cells to receive and transmit an electrical impulse
questions.explanation: Excitability is the cell's capacity to respond at all when stimulated. It's distinct from contractility (actually shortening/contracting) and conductivity (passing the signal along).

questions.question: What is automaticity?
options: Ability of the heart to initiate an electrical impulse on its own
Ability of the heart cells to receive and transmit an electrical impulse
Branches off the bundle of His that conduct impulses to the ventricles
Heart muscle cells returning to their resting electrical state
questions.explanation: Automaticity is what allows the heart to generate its own electrical impulses without needing a signal from the nervous system — it's why a heart can keep beating even outside the body.

questions.question: What are bundle branches?
options: Delays the electrical conduction through the heart
Branches off the bundle of His that conduct impulses to the left and right ventricles
Ability of the heart to initiate an electrical impulse
Distributes electrical impulses from cell to cell throughout the ventricles
questions.explanation: The bundle branches (left and right) split off from the bundle of His and carry the electrical signal down into each ventricle, feeding into the Purkinje fibers from there.

questions.question: What is the SA node?
options: Delays the electrical conduction through the heart
Distributes electrical impulses from cell to cell throughout the ventricles
Initiates the heartbeat
Heart muscle cells return to their resting electrical state
questions.explanation: The SA (sinoatrial) node is the heart's natural pacemaker — it's where each normal heartbeat originates before the signal spreads through the rest of the conduction system.

questions.question: What is contractility?
options: Ability of the heart muscle cells to respond to an impulse or stimulus
Ability of the heart muscle cells to shorten in response to an electrical stimulus
Ability of the heart cells to receive and transmit an electrical impulse
An electrical current that initiates the contraction of the heart muscle
questions.explanation: Contractility is the actual mechanical shortening of the muscle fibers once stimulated — this is the property that turns an electrical signal into an actual pump action.

questions.question: What is depolarization?
options: Heart muscle cells return to their resting electrical state
Delays the electrical conduction through the heart
An electrical current that initiates the contraction of the heart muscle
Ability of the heart to initiate an electrical impulse
questions.explanation: Depolarization is the electrical trigger that sets off muscle contraction. It's the flip side of repolarization, where the cells reset back to their resting state afterward.

questions.question: What is the AV node?
options: Delays the electrical conduction through the heart
Initiates the heartbeat
Distributes electrical impulses from cell to cell throughout the ventricles
Ability of the heart muscle cells to shorten in response to a stimulus
questions.explanation: The AV (atrioventricular) node briefly delays the signal coming from the SA node, giving the atria time to finish contracting and fill the ventricles before ventricular contraction begins.

questions.question: What are Purkinje fibers?
options: Delays the electrical conduction through the heart
Branches off the bundle of His that conduct impulses to the ventricles
Distribute electrical impulses from cell to cell throughout the ventricles
Ability of the heart to initiate an electrical impulse
questions.explanation: Purkinje fibers are the final stage of the conduction pathway — a network that spreads the electrical impulse rapidly across the ventricle walls so the whole chamber contracts together.

questions.question: What is repolarization?
options: An electrical current that initiates the contraction of the heart muscle
Heart muscle cells return to their resting electrical state and the heart muscle relaxes
Ability of the heart to initiate an electrical impulse
Ability of the heart muscle cells to shorten in response to a stimulus
questions.explanation: Repolarization is the recovery phase — cells reset their electrical charge back to baseline so they're ready to be stimulated again for the next beat.

questions.question: What is conductivity, as a property of heart cells?
options: Ability of the heart muscle cells to respond to an impulse or stimulus
Ability of the heart to initiate an electrical impulse
Heart muscle cells return to their resting electrical state
Ability of the heart cells to receive and transmit an electrical impulse
questions.explanation: Conductivity is what lets the electrical signal actually travel from cell to cell throughout the heart — without it, the impulse generated at the SA node would go nowhere.

questions.question: The PR interval is usually:
options: 0.06 to 0.10 second
0.12 to 0.20 second
greater than 0.20 second
less than 0.06 second
questions.explanation: A normal PR interval falls between 0.12 and 0.20 seconds. Anything consistently longer than 0.20 seconds points toward a first-degree AV block.

questions.question: What part of the ECG tracing represents the repolarization of the Purkinje fibers?
options: T wave
PR interval
U wave
P wave
questions.explanation: The U wave, when visible, is thought to represent repolarization of the Purkinje fibers — it's a small, sometimes-absent wave that follows the T wave.

questions.question: What part of the ECG tracing represents the time from the start of atrial activity to the start of ventricular activity?
options: QRS complex
J point
QT interval
PR interval
questions.explanation: The PR interval spans from the very start of the P wave (atrial depolarization) to the start of the QRS complex (ventricular depolarization) — capturing that atria-to-ventricle handoff.

questions.question: What part of the ECG tracing is measured from the end of the S wave to the beginning of the T wave, and is normally on the isoelectric line?
options: ST segment
QT segment
U wave
QRS complex
questions.explanation: The ST segment sits between ventricular depolarization (the QRS complex) and repolarization (the T wave). Elevation or depression of this segment is a key marker physicians watch for in cardiac events.

questions.question: What wave on the ECG tracing is not always seen, and when seen can sometimes indicate an electrolyte imbalance?
options: U wave
P wave
Q wave
R wave
questions.explanation: The U wave is inconsistent — it isn't always present, but when it becomes prominent it can be a clue toward things like low potassium (hypokalemia).

questions.question: The heart's ability to create its own electrical impulse is known as:
options: Conductivity
Contractility
Automaticity
Excitability
questions.explanation: Automaticity is specifically about self-generating an impulse without outside stimulation — it's what makes cardiac tissue unique compared to most other muscle in the body.

questions.question: When stimulated, the sympathetic system of the body causes the heart rate to:
options: increase
decrease
remain the same
increase, then decrease
questions.explanation: The sympathetic nervous system is the body's 'fight or flight' response — activating it speeds up the heart rate. The parasympathetic system (via the vagus nerve) does the opposite, slowing it down.

questions.question: Which vessel of the body contains the highest concentration of oxygen?
options: Aorta
Pulmonary artery
Superior vena cava
Inferior vena cava
questions.explanation: The aorta carries freshly oxygenated blood straight from the left ventricle out to the body. The vena cava vessels and the pulmonary artery all carry deoxygenated blood, on their way back to or toward the lungs.

questions.question: The heart is contained inside a sac also known as the:
options: endocardium
pericardial sac
myocardium sac
fluid sac
questions.explanation: The pericardial sac (pericardium) is the protective double-layered membrane surrounding the heart. The endocardium and myocardium are internal layers of the heart wall itself, not the outer sac.

questions.question: Which of the following would most likely decrease the heart rate?
options: Stress, exercise, and fever
High concentration of potassium in the blood
Decreased blood pressure
Increased blood pressure
questions.explanation: Increased blood pressure triggers baroreceptors that signal the body to slow the heart rate back down. Stress, exercise, fever, and decreased blood pressure all tend to push heart rate up instead.

questions.question: What is deoxygenated blood?
options: Blood having oxygen
Blood that has little or no oxygen
Circulation of blood through the heart muscle
Circulation between the heart and the entire body
questions.explanation: Deoxygenated blood is what returns to the right side of the heart from the body, having already given up its oxygen to the tissues — it's then sent to the lungs to be replenished.

questions.question: What is the cardiac cycle?
options: The period from the beginning of one beat of the heart to the beginning of the next
Contraction phase of the cardiac cycle, when the heart is pumping blood out to the body
Phase of the cardiac cycle when the heart is expanding and refilling
Circulation of blood through the heart and heart muscle
questions.explanation: The cardiac cycle is the full sequence — one complete round of systole (contraction) and diastole (relaxation/refilling) — measured from one heartbeat's start to the next.

questions.question: What is systole?
options: Phase of the cardiac cycle when the heart is expanding and refilling; also known as the relaxation phase
Contraction phase of the cardiac cycle, when the heart is pumping blood out to the body
The period from the beginning of one beat of the heart to the beginning of the next
Transportation of blood between the heart and lungs
questions.explanation: Systole is the contraction phase — the moment the ventricles squeeze and eject blood out into the aorta and pulmonary trunk. Diastole, its counterpart, is the relaxation and refilling phase.

questions.question: What is coronary circulation?
options: Circulation of blood through the heart and heart muscle
Circulation between the heart and the entire body, excluding the lungs
Transportation of blood between the heart and lungs
Blood that has little or no oxygen
questions.explanation: Coronary circulation refers specifically to the blood supply feeding the heart muscle itself, via the coronary arteries — distinct from systemic circulation, which supplies the rest of the body.

questions.question: What is systemic circulation?
options: Transportation of blood between the heart and lungs
Circulation of blood through the heart and heart muscle
Circulation between the heart and the entire body, excluding the lungs
Blood having oxygen
questions.explanation: Systemic circulation is the large loop that carries oxygenated blood out to every organ and tissue in the body (except the lungs) and returns deoxygenated blood back to the heart.

questions.question: What is oxygenated blood?
options: Blood that has little or no oxygen
Blood having oxygen
Circulation between the heart and lungs
Contraction phase of the cardiac cycle
questions.explanation: Oxygenated blood is blood that's picked up oxygen in the lungs and is ready to deliver it to the body's tissues — it's what fills the left side of the heart and the aorta.

questions.question: What is diastole?
options: Contraction phase of the cardiac cycle, when the heart is pumping blood out to the body
The period from the beginning of one beat of the heart to the beginning of the next
Phase of the cardiac cycle when the heart is expanding and refilling; also known as the relaxation phase
Circulation between the heart and the entire body
questions.explanation: Diastole is the relaxation phase, when the heart chambers expand and refill with blood in preparation for the next contraction (systole).

questions.question: What is pulmonary circulation?
options: Circulation of blood through the heart and heart muscle
Circulation between the heart and the entire body, excluding the lungs
Blood having oxygen
Transportation of blood between the heart and lungs
questions.explanation: Pulmonary circulation is the shorter loop that carries deoxygenated blood from the right ventricle to the lungs to pick up oxygen, then returns oxygenated blood to the left atrium.
